hero.notFoundExplanation: We may not have that location in our database, or it may not yet be connected to our disclosure data. Please try searching again.

description.text: Browse local climate hazards, actions, and adaption projects seeking funding from cities, states, and regions worldwide.

welcomeModal.mobileNotice: Note: this experience has not been optimized for mobile. For best results, please use the CDP Adaptation & Action Explorer on desktop.
welcomeModal.title: CDP Adaptation & Action Explorer
welcomeModal.description: An open-source tool helping cities, states, and regions understand climate risks, explore resilience solutions, and communicate progress to partners and funders. More features are on the way.

maps.noHazardData: No map data available for this hazard

hazards.gdpLabel: Total GDP at Risk in USD

noHazardsContent.title: {{location}} hasn't disclosed hazard information for their jurisdiction as of the {{year}} CDP disclosure
noHazardsContent.description:
Currently there isn’t any hazard information in the {{year}} CDP disclosure from {{location}}. There could be a few reasons for this, but for the sake of trust, accuracy and quality we aren’t showing other information.

As this project matures we are hoping to bring in previous years disclosures if they exist. We also want to apply methodologies and trusted external hazard data sources to compute hazards that could impact this location, as well as other locations globally. You can view other disclosed data for this location in the Government actions tab for goals, actions and other projects for {{location}}.

hazardOverview.title: Hazard overview
hazardOverview.topHazardsTooltip: Hazards are ranked by how likely they are to occur and how severe their impact could be. This shows the top four — there may be other disclosed hazards not included in this list.
hazardOverview.mostExposedSectorsTooltip: Sectors are derived from disclosures and sorted by number of mentions or alphabetically. This reflects only what has been disclosed and does not indicate relative importance or completeness.

noSolutionsContent.title: {{location}} hasn't disclosed hazard information for their jurisdiction as of the {{year}} CDP disclosure
noSolutionsContent.description:
Currently there isn’t any hazard information in the {{year}} CDP disclosure from {{location}}. There could be a few reasons for this, but for the sake of trust, accuracy and quality we aren’t showing other information.

Since we don't have hazard data we can’t compute comparable locations that are affected by similar environmental hazards. What you could see here, and other locations in this product have solutions here, is a list of categories of solutions that are implemented other places around the world that share similar hazard profiles.

Solutions are categorized actions that could be applied at other locations around the world. Enabling users to look at their location and see what others are doing around the world that could be applied or implemented here.

methodology.title: Methodology
methodology.intro: This platform draws from both CDP's annual questionnaire for reporting by governments and supplemental hazard data to cover the climate hazards that cities, states, and regions face and how they are addressing them. It also surfaces insights from around the globe by identifying relevant solutions and actions across cities with similar characteristics.

questionnaire.title: CDP's Annual Questionnaire
questionnaire.body: Cities, states and regions are invited to complete CDP's annual questionnaire through the CDP-ICLEI Track disclosure portal. The questionnaire captures qualitative and quantitative information on topics including governance, climate risk and vulnerability assessments, greenhouse gas emissions inventories, energy consumption, sectoral data, adaptation and mitigation goals, and climate planning and actions. Jurisdictions can update their disclosures through questionnaires released yearly. The Explorer currently displays data from 2025 public disclosures. Additional information and most recent questions can be found here.

hazards.title: Hazards
hazards.intro: For jurisdictions that have disclosed information through the questionnaire, hazard data is primarily drawn from this disclosure. Additional hazard information and data for visualization is sourced from remote sensing and model-derived data accessed through Google Earth Engine.

questionnaireData.title: Cities, States, and Regions Questionnaire Data
questionnaireData.p1: Cities, states, and regions are asked to share details on the most significant climate hazards facing their jurisdiction (Question 2.2 in the 2025 questionnaire). The self-disclosed hazard data may contain information about vulnerable population and sectoral exposure, hazard probability, magnitude of impact, expected hazard changes in intensity or frequency, and the timeframe of expected change. Probability and magnitude responses are given along a five category scale: low, medium-low, medium, medium-high, and high.
questionnaireData.p2: For each jurisdiction, up to four top hazards are displayed on the platform, primarily ranked based on the average value of their reported probability and magnitude. Proportion of the population exposed to the hazard is used where data is incomplete.

geeData.title: Google Earth Engine and External Data
geeData.p1: To provide additional context and better understand the dynamics of a hazard in a given jurisdiction, geospatial hazard data layers were developed from datasets available on Google Earth Engine and other data portals, including from specific risk indices or hazard forecasting models, as well as directly from downscaled climate projections.
geeData.p2: Earth Engine and other external hazard data were rescaled to values ranging from 0 to 5, aligning it for easier comparison with the five-category, low to high questionnaire response format scale. A value of 0 represents when a hazard is not expected to impact the jurisdiction within the next 5 years. Jurisdictions without any exposure to the given hazard were omitted in this calculation, allowing the analysis to better reflect comparisons across areas actually experiencing the hazard.
geeData.p3: Many hazard layers are available for both historical baseline conditions as well as future projections. Projected values are provided for two-decade ranges, centered on three milestone years: 2030, 2050, and 2080. Hazard projections are provided under four combined, widely used socioeconomic-climate scenarios. These scenarios represent increasingly extreme warming pathways: SSP1–RCP2.6, SSP2-4.5, SSP3–RCP7.0, and SSP5–RCP8.5. There are a few exceptions: soil erosion is only available for historical conditions, and flooding hazards are available for historical conditions, RCP 4.5, and RCP 8.5 scenarios (former climate scenario characterizations). Water stress includes three of the four scenarios, omitting SSP2-4.5.
geeData.p4: For fire weather, extreme heat/heat stress, extreme cold, and extreme precipitation, downscaled climate projections from NEX-GDDP-CMIP6 were used. Hazard values were calculated for ~28-km grid cells for each year under the various scenarios. A multi-model ensemble (MME) median value was calculated for each cell using all available climate models. This provides a central, representative value across diverse climate model projections and reduces the effects of variation between models.

layers.title: Learn more about the data used for each hazard layer:
layers.footnote: Additional hazard layers may be added over time.

hazards.outro: To display hazard maps and assign hazard values for each jurisdiction, global hazard layers from Google Earth Engine were filtered by a jurisdiction's administrative boundaries and spatially averaged within them. Administrative boundaries were obtained primarily through data from Overture Maps Foundation. When unavailable, boundaries were obtained from OpenStreetMap or datasets available on the UN OCHA Humanitarian Data Exchange. When no appropriate jurisdiction boundary was found, pseudo-boundaries were created using the jurisdiction's geographic center (sourced from the above or manually in Google Maps) and their reported land area.

waterStress.title: Water stress
waterStress.description: Global water risk data was sourced from the World Resources Institute (WRI) Aqueduct Water Risk Atlas 4.0 framework and aggregated to the HydroBASINS Level 6 unit. The values are provided from WRI in the scale of 0-5.

flooding.title: Fluvial (riverine) and coastal flooding
flooding.description: Fluvial and coastal flood depth was sourced from the World Resources Institute Aqueduct Flood Hazard Map dataset. Data is provided at a 1-km resolution for 100-year (i.e. 1 in 100 probability of occurring in a given year) floods, under a high sea level rise scenario and accounting for subsidence. These were calculated under RCPs (representative concentration pathways), which were the formerly recognized ways to describe climate trajectories.

fireWeather.title: Fire weather
fireWeather.description: This draws from NASA's Fire Weather Index (FWI) dataset, which is based on NEX-GDDP-CMIP6 projections. FWI represents the relationship between weather conditions and elevated fire risk. The number of days per year in which the FWI exceeds a threshold of 45 is used, representing days with high fire danger.

extremeHeat.title: Extreme heat / heat stress
extremeHeat.description: This is calculated as the number of days on which the near surface air temperature (the temperature felt at 2 meters above the ground) exceeds 35 °C, representing extreme heat-day frequency.

extremeCold.title: Extreme cold
extremeCold.description: This is calculated as the number of days on which the minimum near surface air temperature (the temperature felt at 2 meters above the ground) falls below 0 °C, representing extreme cold-day frequency.

extremePrecipitation.title: Extreme precipitation
extremePrecipitation.description: This is calculated as the maximum 5-day precipitation total in a given year, representing extreme precipitation events.

soilDegradation.title: Soil degradation / erosion
soilDegradation.description: To represent one aspect of soil erosion, global landslide hazard maps were accessed from the World Bank's Global Landslide Hazard Map. This is available at 1-km resolution.

governmentActions.title: Government Actions
governmentActions.intro: This tab contains three categories of information from the disclosure:
governmentActions.listGoals: Adaptation Goals (Question 5.1 in the questionnaire)
governmentActions.goals: Adaptation Goals are adaptation targets based on climate hazards and are typically qualitatively defined.
governmentActions.actions: Actions are defined here as the interventions taken to achieve adaptation goals and carry out a given strategy, which can include policies, projects, programs, partnerships, and more. Adaptation action data may include information on the sectors it impacts, attributes of resilience it enhances, co-benefits of the project, population affected, expectations for enhanced resilience timeframe, and funding sources and costs.
governmentActions.projects: Projects are specific climate-related initiatives for which jurisdictions are seeking funding, as reported via the questionnaire. Information about projects may include funding needs, project description, financing models, and more.
governmentActions.outro: In addition, the Government Actions tab indicates whether the jurisdiction has completed a Climate Risk and Vulnerability Assessment, or has disclosed that they plan to do so.

solutions.title: Action Ideas
solutions.p1: This tab presents information on relevant actions being taken by peer jurisdictions, derived from actions reported in the questionnaire. Jurisdictions highlighted here are those in the same biome as a given city, state, or region, with actions that address one or more of the top 4 hazards of the given city, state, or region. Action Ideas highlight the types of actions that jurisdictions are taking to adapt to climate hazards. Cities, states, and regions choose from an extensive list of solutions that represents a given action when reporting the actions they are taking.
solutions.p2: These relevant actions from other jurisdictions in the same ecoregion are surfaced to help users explore what others are doing to adapt to climate hazards. Actions from these jurisdictions are collated and then grouped by solution category (e.g. engineered and built environment actions, behavioural actions) and solution type (e.g. sea walls and coastal protection structures, genetic techniques). The full list of solution types can be found under section 9.1 in the CDP questionnaire guide. From this list, the top 25 solutions are chosen based on those with the highest number of associated actions reported. A maximum of 5 solutions are chosen from a given solution category to highlight a diversity of efforts. For each solution, a subset of actions are selected to display as examples.

minimumCriteria.title: Minimum criteria: Data cleaning
minimumCriteria.intro: As part of the data cleaning process, minimum criteria were defined for self-reported data displayed in the CDP Adaptation and Action Explorer to ensure usability, credibility, and comparability. These criteria establish a baseline level of data quality, allowing incomplete, inconsistent, or otherwise unreliable responses to be filtered out prior to analysis.

climateHazards.criteria: The climate hazard reported includes information on the type of hazard, the sectors most exposed by the hazard, and a description of the impacts on vulnerable populations and sectors.

adaptationGoals.criteria: The adaptation goal has a title or description, a base year, and a target year greater than or equal to the reporting year (2025).

adaptationActions.criteria: The action reported includes information on the type and category of action, the climate hazards that the action addresses, a description of the action, and the sectors the action applies to.

projects.criteria:
The project reported includes information on the project area and at least one more datapoint:
  • Project title
  • Project stage
  • Financing status
  • Financing model
  • Project description
  • Total cost
  • Investment needed

learnMore.title: About the Explorer
learnMore.intro: Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. The CDP Adaptation & Action Explorer brings together fragmented climate disclosure, hazard, and resilience information into a single workspace so subnational governments can find what matters fastest.
